docs.text: RESTify Experiment Replication Package All you need to replicate our findings, in one place. About As part of our submission to the ESEM 2023 conference , we offer a replication package that allows fast and independent replication of all our results and interpretations. This page serves as starting point for the various components needed. Note: This is the anonymized version of the replication package, created specifically for the reviewers. In case of an accept we will provide a link to the original replication package. We stress that the anonymization process affects the replicability, e.g. when java packages were renamed or parts of links were anonymized. Package Content This section hot-links package content by scope Experiment Setup Recruitment Material : A reference to the webpage used for participant recruitment. MiniMax Heuristic Implementation for Balanced Groups : Source code and documentation of the algorithm implemented to create a balanced group allocation, that is to say analyze participant self-declared skills and create four comparable groups. Task Instruction Material : Collection with instructions for the task instructions. There are four, since the experiment had four groups with diverging task context and order. Sample Task Applications : The sample applications used task illustration and the conversion task. Data Collected and Analyzed Raw Experiment Collected Data : CSV, number/excel and text files that conclude the raw data collected throughout the experiment. Submission Correctness Evaluation Tool : Sources and documentation of the analyzer tool that we implemented to assess correctness of participant submissions. Extracted Times and Video Observations : CSV and textual summary files that conclude data extracted from video material viewing, such as transcript of observations and time measurements for the individual conversion tasks. Statistical Analysis of Findings Interactive Jupyter Notebook for Analysis Replication : Center piece of our replication package. You can recreate all graphics using an interactive notebook that works on the aforementioned raw data and creates the same figures and listings as included in our submission.
docs.title: Welcome

docs.text: All you need to replicate our findings, in one place.

docs.text: Submission Correctness Analyzer This page contains links to the tools needed to replicate our test results. You will also need the raw participant submission source code to test. The test chain for assessing all submissions is fully automated. A bash script iterates over all submissions and all test endpoints, and compares execution time behaviour to the stipulated interface behaviour. It afterwards creates a massive CSV file, for further analysis. The CSV is e.g. consumed by the interactive notebook that creates the same figures as printed in our article submission. You can also directly download the resulting CSV, or inspect the repositories below to replicate the CSV content by running the test toolchain on your local machine. More details on the technical functioning of the test chain are provided in the Repo documentation. Note that the script internally calls java unit tests, which are hosted in extra repositories, for they are application specific. Link to anonymized automated test chain: RestifyAnalyzer Link to anonymized BookStore REST unit tests: BookStoreRestTest Link to anonymized Xox REST unit tests: XoxRestTest
docs.title: Correctness Analyzer

docs.text: Sample Refactoring Applications Throughout the experiment we used three sample applications, here you find references to the source code and documentation of these applications: The Zoo. Which we created ourselves, to illustrate the experiment conversion task. It is minimal: Anonymized Sources on GitHub Anonymized JavaDoc on GitHub The BookStore. It was created by the original authors of the DSL conversion toolchain as demo application. We here only refer to the original sources and documentation: Original BookStore Sources on GitHub Original BookStore JavaDoc on GitHub Tic Tac Toe. It was created by imitating project structure of the BookStore, to obtain a second sample of comparable magnitude. We here provide the anonymized sources and documentation Anonymized Xox Sources on GitHub Anonymized Xox JavaDoc on GitHub
docs.title: Applications

docs.text: Raw Collected Data Here you can download the participant data, collected after experiment completion. There is a total of 28 participants, in four groups. Every identified pair of colour and animal is unique, e.g. red squid is a different participant than green squid . You will find 2 projects for each of the 28 participants, as well as textual participant feedback. * A manually converted application. Sources as a maven project. * A tool assisted converted application. Contains the models created and the corresponding generated sources. Download Links Zip file with all anonymized participant submissions . About 1MB, does not contain compiled binaries, only project sources. Cleaned from identifiers, for double blinded. Contains individual participant feedback. MD5 Checksum: d2f30841c57e55c708671bf9a7a0a6aa . Participant feedback extracted: Mac Numbers File Microsoft Excel File CSV file Participant feedback statistics: CSV file with most significant findings across all textual feedback. How to test You first need to locally install the bookstore and xox internals as local maven libraries. See the referenced projects for instructions . Afterwards you can compile and run participant submissions with maven: cd next to a pom.xml file. Compile and start the project with mvn clean package spring-boot:run Note: You will need Java and Maven installed on your system to test participant submissions.
docs.title: Raw Data

docs.text: Task Instructions Once accepted to the experiment and allocated to a group, every participant received an email with a personal pseudonym and a link to a detailed instruction webpage. Some links on the instruction page are broken, due to the anonymization process, as the URL would reveal our identity. This also affects the instruction video screencasts. Here are anonymized versions of the task instructions pages, for the four groups: Red Group Green Group Blue Group Yellow Group Note that the task context and order changes, depending on which group a participant adheres to.
docs.title: Instructions

docs.title: Task Instructions

docs.text: MiniMax Experiment Group Auto Balancer We implemented a custom MiniMax Heuristic, to assess the participant's skill profiles and create balanced experiment groups. To maintain transparency we provide source code and documentation of the heuristic used to create the participant allocations. The program parses all provided self assessment forms (see corresponding entry of replication bundle ) which they provided during recruitment, and then performs a MiniMax search for the fairest group allocation. A side product of this software is is the generation of personalized emails for following communication with the individual participants. Since the purpose of this program is to select of all applicants and provide a mapping from their legal names to pseudonyms, it can be only executed with access to the participant details. The latter we cannot release for legal reasons. Source Code An anonymized version of the MiniMax implementation and documentation is available on GitHub, for the reviewers consideration.
docs.title: MiniMax Heuristic

docs.text: Jupyter Notebook to Replicate all Statistics All statistical figures and listings in our paper submission were created programmatically. We provide: The raw data The code used for statistical analysis The documentation Anyone can replicate the correctness of our claims. We provide several access points, for fast to thorough replication: Static, rendered Jupyter Notebook: You can inspect a static one-shot render of our Notebook here, on GitHub. You only need a browser. Please not that the anonymization process damaged some of the integrated figures. We apologize for the inconvenience. Dynamic, using a docker configuration. See install instructions for Docker . Dynamic, as a Jupyter Notebook. See install instructions for native Jupyter Dynamic, using the raw python sources. See install instructions for PyCharm Statistics listing For a description of all functionality offered by the programmed statistical analysis, see the cell description table . This likewise contains links to the source code for specific analyses performed.
docs.title: Interactive Notebook

docs.text: Compiled Experiment Observations We collected more than 72 hours of video onscreen recordings throughout the experiment. Participants were asked to avoid capturing personal information or identifiers. Unfortunately this request was widely ignored. To preserve participant anonymity, we therefore cannot provide the original video material . We can however provide several information, extracted from the video material: Task solving and task preparation times: We measured how much time every participant spent on the actual project conversion tasks and the instructions and provide precise information on their time spendings. Task deviations, difficulties, remarkable observations: We provide for each particiant and each refactoring task a transcript of all noteworthy events. This includes problems with specific task phases, problems with software used, even information on their task solving activity itself. Download Links You can download the time measurements and observations and bundled files: General time and observation data grids: Mac Numbers File Microsoft Excel File CSV File Summary of common observations and additional details: Text File
docs.title: Times and Observations
